questions.questionText: The volume of air inspired or expired with each normal breath is the:
questions.explanation: Normal tidal volume is approximately 500 mL in an adult.

questions.questionText: Which of the following cannot be measured by simple spirometry?
questions.explanation: RV remains in the lungs after maximal expiration and requires helium dilution or plethysmography.

questions.questionText: Surfactant is produced by which cells in the alveoli?
questions.explanation: Type II pneumocytes synthesize and secrete pulmonary surfactant.

questions.questionText: The primary function of pulmonary surfactant is to:
questions.explanation: Surfactant reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse (atelectasis).

questions.questionText: Most oxygen in the blood is transported:
questions.explanation: Approximately 98% of oxygen is carried bound to iron in hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: Most carbon dioxide in the blood is transported as:
questions.explanation: About 70% of CO2 is carried as bicarbonate formed in the RBC by carbonic anhydrase.

questions.questionText: A rightward shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve means:
questions.optionB: Increased affinity of Hb for oxygen
questions.optionC: Decreased affinity of Hb for oxygen
questions.explanation: A right shift facilitates oxygen unloading to tissues (e.g., in acidosis, hypercapnia, fever).

questions.questionText: The central chemoreceptors in the medulla are primarily stimulated by:
questions.optionA: An increase in H+ concentration in the CSF
questions.explanation: CO2 crosses the blood-brain barrier, forms H+, which stimulates the central chemoreceptors.

questions.questionText: The peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid and aortic bodies) are most sensitive to:
questions.explanation: Only peripheral chemoreceptors respond to hypoxia (especially when PaO2 < 60 mmHg).

questions.questionText: In a forced expiratory volume (FEV1) test, a normal FEV1/FVC ratio is approximately:
questions.explanation: A healthy person can exhale about 80% of their vital capacity in the first second.

questions.questionText: In Respiratory System, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Physiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Physiology references for Respiratory System. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Respiratory System.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Respiratory System scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Respiratory System.
